title: AutoML & Hyperparameter Optimization
description: Grid/random search, Bayesian optimization, Gaussian processes, TPE, Hyperband, BOHB, population-based training, neural architecture search, and meta-learning for HPO.
details: AutoML automates the process of selecting and tuning machine learning models — hyperparameters, architectures, preprocessing pipelines, and even inference heuristics. This file covers hyperparameter optimization (grid search, random search, Bayesian optimization with Gaussian Processes and Tree-structured Parzen Estimators), multi-fidelity methods (Hyperband, Successive Halving, BOHB), population-based training (PBT, PB2), neural architecture search (NAS with RL, DARTS, ENAS, ProxylessNAS, evolution), Auto-WEKA, Auto-sklearn, AutoGluon, meta-learning for warm-starting HPO (learning curve extrapolation, dataset meta-features), and practical tips (search spaces, early stopping, parallelization). AutoML is a growing interview topic, especially for ML platform and ML infrastructure roles.

sections.title: Bayesian Optimization for HPO
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Instead of trying random hyperparameters blindly, build a probabilistic model (surrogate) of the objective function. Use this model to decide which hyperparameters to evaluate next — trading off exploration (high uncertainty) and exploitation (high predicted performance).

GP-based Bayesian Optimization:

  • Surrogate model: Gaussian Process with a kernel (usually Matern 5/2 or RBF). GP gives both predicted performance μ(x) and uncertainty σ(x) for any hyperparameter setting x.
  • Acquisition function: Decides which x to evaluate next.
    • Expected Improvement (EI): EI(x) = E[max(0, f(x) - f(x+))]. x+ = best observed value. Closed form: EI(x) = (μ(x) - f(x+)) Φ(Z) + σ(x) φ(Z) where Z = (μ(x) - f(x+)) / σ(x).
    • Upper Confidence Bound (UCB): UCB(x) = μ(x) + κ σ(x). κ controls exploration-exploitation tradeoff.
    • Probability of Improvement (PI): P(f(x) ≥ f(x+) + ξ). Less common.
  • Fit GP → optimize acquisition → evaluate → update GP → repeat.

TPE (Tree-structured Parzen Estimator):

  • Instead of modeling P(score | params), model P(params | score) and P(params | not score).
  • Split observed points into “good” and “bad” by a quantile threshold γ (typically γ=0.15, top 15% are good).
  • Fit two density estimates: l(x) = P(x | good), g(x) = P(x | bad).
  • EI(x) ∝ l(x) / g(x). Sample from l(x) and g(x), choose point with highest ratio.
  • Better than GP for high-dimensional or mixed discrete/continuous spaces. Used in Hyperopt, Optuna.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import norm
from sklearn.gaussian_process import GaussianProcessRegressor
from sklearn.gaussian_process.kernels import Matern

# === Expected Improvement acquisition function ===
def expected_improvement(mu, sigma, y_best, xi=0.01):
    """
    mu: predicted mean at candidate x
    sigma: predicted std at candidate x
    y_best: best observed value (maximization)
    xi: exploration-exploitation parameter (higher = more exploration)
    """
    if sigma == 0:
        return 0
    Z = (mu - y_best - xi) / sigma
    ei = (mu - y_best - xi) * norm.cdf(Z) + sigma * norm.pdf(Z)
    return ei

# === Bayesian Optimization loop ===
class BayesianOptimization:
    def __init__(self, bounds, n_init=10, kernel=Matern(nu=2.5)):
        """
        bounds: list of (low, high) for each dimension
        """
        self.bounds = np.array(bounds)
        self.dim = len(bounds)
        self.gp = GaussianProcessRegressor(
            kernel=kernel, alpha=1e-6,
            normalize_y=True, n_restarts_optimizer=5
        )
        self.X = []  # evaluated points
        self.y = []  # evaluated scores
        self.n_init = n_init
    
    def suggest(self):
        """Return next hyperparameter candidate."""
        if len(self.X) < self.n_init:
            # Random initialization
            return np.random.uniform(
                self.bounds[:, 0], self.bounds[:, 1], size=self.dim
            )
        
        # Fit GP
        self.gp.fit(np.array(self.X), np.array(self.y))
        y_best = max(self.y)
        
        # Optimize acquisition function via random candidates + L-BFGS
        n_candidates = 1000
        candidates = np.random.uniform(
            self.bounds[:, 0], self.bounds[:, 1],
            size=(n_candidates, self.dim)
        )
        mu, sigma = self.gp.predict(candidates, return_std=True)
        
        ei = expected_improvement(mu, sigma, y_best)
        best_idx = np.argmax(ei)
        
        # Local optimization around best candidate
        from scipy.optimize import minimize
        def ei_negative(x):
            mu, sigma = self.gp.predict(x.reshape(1, -1), return_std=True)
            return -expected_improvement(mu, sigma, y_best)
        
        result = minimize(
            ei_negative,
            candidates[best_idx],
            bounds=self.bounds,
            method='L-BFGS-B'
        )
        return result.x
    
    def update(self, x, y):
        self.X.append(x)
        self.y.append(y)

# === TPE (simplified) ===
class TPE:
    def __init__(self, bounds, gamma=0.15):
        self.bounds = bounds
        self.gamma = gamma
        self.X = []
        self.y = []
    
    def suggest(self, n_samples=100):
        if len(self.X) < 5:
            return np.random.uniform(
                self.bounds[:, 0], self.bounds[:, 1], size=len(self.bounds)
            )
        
        # Split into good/bad
        quantile = np.quantile(self.y, 1 - self.gamma)
        X_good = [x for x, y in zip(self.X, self.y) if y >= quantile]
        X_bad = [x for x, y in zip(self.X, self.y) if y < quantile]
        
        # Kernel density estimation for l(x) and g(x)
        from sklearn.neighbors import KernelDensity
        l_kde = KernelDensity(kernel='gaussian').fit(X_good)
        g_kde = KernelDensity(kernel='gaussian').fit(X_bad)
        
        # Sample candidates, pick best ratio l(x)/g(x)
        candidates = np.random.uniform(
            self.bounds[:, 0], self.bounds[:, 1],
            size=(n_samples, len(self.bounds))
        )
        l_score = l_kde.score_samples(candidates)
        g_score = g_kde.score_samples(candidates)
        ratio = np.exp(l_score - g_score)
        
        return candidates[np.argmax(ratio)]
    
    def update(self, x, y):
        self.X.append(x)
        self.y.append(y)

sections.title: Multi-Fidelity: Hyperband & BOHB
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Bayesian optimization evaluates full training runs — expensive. Multi-fidelity methods train many configurations for a few epochs (low budget), prune bad ones early, and only fully train the most promising ones.

Successive Halving:

  • Start with N configurations, train each for budget b (e.g., 1 epoch). Keep top 1/η (e.g., η=3 means keep top 33%). Train survivors for η × b. Repeat.
  • Problem: How to choose N and the number of rounds? Too few rounds → bad allocation. Too many → waste on bad configs early.

Hyperband:

  • Runs multiple brackets of Successive Halving with different (N, budget) tradeoffs. “One bracket starts with many configs and small budgets, another starts with few configs and large budgets.”
  • Guarantees: has strong theoretical guarantees (no-regret property). Empirically: finds good configs 5–10× faster than random search.
  • Drawback: treats all configurations equally (no learning across evaluations).

BOHB (Bayesian Optimization + Hyperband):

  • Replaces random sampling in Hyperband with TPE-based sampling.
  • At each budget level, use TPE on observations at that budget to suggest promising configurations. Fits separate density estimators per budget level.
  • Combines the best of both: fast pruning (Hyperband) + learning from results (BO).
  • Consistently outperforms both Hyperband and BO individually.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score

# === Successive Halving ===
def successive_halving(objective, configs, budget_per_config, eta=3):
    """
    objective: function(config, budget) -> score
    configs: list of hyperparameter dicts
    budget_per_config: total budget per config (e.g., epochs)
    """
    n_configs = len(configs)
    budget = budget_per_config / (eta ** int(np.log(n_configs) / np.log(eta)))
    
    while budget <= budget_per_config and n_configs >= 1:
        scores = []
        for cfg in configs:
            score = objective(cfg, min(budget, budget_per_config))
            scores.append(score)
        
        # Keep top 1/eta
        n_keep = max(1, n_configs // eta)
        idx = np.argsort(scores)[-n_keep:]  # maximize
        configs = [configs[i] for i in idx]
        n_configs = len(configs)
        budget *= eta
    
    return configs

# === Hyperband ===
def hyperband(objective, max_budget, eta=3, R=None):
    """
    max_budget: maximum budget per config
    eta: halving factor
    """
    s_max = int(np.log(max_budget) / np.log(eta))
    B = (s_max + 1) * max_budget  # total budget
    
    best_config = None
    best_score = -float('inf')
    
    for s in reversed(range(s_max + 1)):
        n_configs = int(np.ceil(B / (max_budget * (s + 1))))
        r = max_budget / (eta ** s)
        
        # Randomly sample configurations
        configs = [sample_random_config() for _ in range(n_configs)]
        
        for i in range(s + 1):
            n_keep = max(1, n_configs // eta)
            budget = r * (eta ** i)
            
            scores = [objective(cfg, budget) for cfg in configs]
            
            idx = np.argsort(scores)[-n_keep:]
            configs = [configs[i] for i in idx]
            n_configs = len(configs)
        
        # Final evaluation
        score = objective(configs[0], max_budget)
        if score > best_score:
            best_score = score
            best_config = configs[0]
    
    return best_config, best_score

# === Population Based Training (PBT) ===
class PBT:
    """
    Train a population of models in parallel.
    Periodically: (1) copy weights from better models (exploit).
    (2) perturb hyperparameters (explore).
    """
    def __init__(self, population_size=20, fraction_exploit=0.2):
        self.pop_size = population_size
        self.fraction_exploit = fraction_exploit
        self.population = []  # list of (model, hparams, score)
    
    def initialize(self, model_class, hparam_space):
        for _ in range(self.pop_size):
            hparams = {k: v['sampler']() for k, v in hparam_space.items()}
            model = model_class(**hparams)
            self.population.append({'model': model, 'hparams': hparams, 'score': 0})
    
    def step(self, data_iter):
        # Train each model for one step
        for member in self.population:
            loss = train_step(member['model'], data_iter)
            member['score'] = evaluate(member['model'])
        
        # Sort by performance
        self.population.sort(key=lambda m: m['score'], reverse=True)
        
        # Exploit & explore: replace bottom fraction with mutated top
        n_replace = int(self.pop_size * self.fraction_exploit)
        for i in range(-n_replace, 0):
            # Copy from a top performer
            donor = self.population[np.random.randint(self.pop_size - n_replace)]
            self.population[i]['model'].load_state_dict(
                donor['model'].state_dict()
            )
            # Perturb hyperparameters (multiplicative, log-uniform)
            for k, v in self.population[i]['hparams'].items():
                if np.random.random() < 0.2:  # 20% mutation prob
                    v *= 2 ** np.random.uniform(-1, 1)  # multiply by 0.5-2
                    self.population[i]['hparams'][k] = v

sections.title: Neural Architecture Search (NAS)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Instead of hand-engineering neural architectures, search over the space of possible architectures (connections, operations, channels). NAS automates the most tedious part of deep learning research.

Search strategies:

  • Evolutionary NAS: Population of architectures. Mutate (change operation, add/remove connection) and crossover (combine parts of two architectures). Tournament selection. Used in AmoebaNet, regularized evolution.
  • RL-based NAS: RNN controller generates architecture description as a sequence of tokens. Controller reward = validation accuracy after training. Update via policy gradient (REINFORCE). Used in NASNet, MNASNet.
  • Weight-sharing / One-shot NAS: Train a supernetwork containing all possible operations. Each architecture is a subgraph of the supernetwork. No need to train from scratch. Used in ENAS, DARTS, ProxylessNAS.
  • DARTS (Differentiable Architecture Search): Make the discrete architecture search continuous via softmax over operations. ō(i,j)(x) = Σ softmax(αo) · o(x). Learn architecture weights α via bilevel optimization: minα Lval(w*(α), α) s.t. w*(α) = argminw Ltrain(w, α).

Practical considerations:

  • Search space design is more important than the search algorithm: macro vs micro (cell-based) search.
  • Proxy tasks: train on a subset (CIFAR-10), transfer to ImageNet.
  • Cost: DARTS ~4 GPU-days, ENAS ~0.5 GPU-days, regularized evolution ~2000 GPU-days for AmoebaNet.
  • Reproducibility: NAS results are sensitive to hyperparameters. Random search in a well-designed space is a strong baseline.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# === DARTS differentiable architecture search ===
# Simplified: search between two operations per edge

class MixedOp(nn.Module):
    """Mixed operation weighted by learnable architecture parameters."""
    def __init__(self, in_channels, out_channels):
        super().__init__()
        self.ops = nn.ModuleList([
            nn.Identity() if in_channels == out_channels else nn.Conv2d(in_channels, out_channels, 1),
            nn.Sequential(
                nn.Conv2d(in_channels, out_channels, 3, padding=1, bias=False),
                nn.BatchNorm2d(out_channels),
                nn.ReLU()
            ),
            nn.Sequential(
                nn.Conv2d(in_channels, out_channels, 5, padding=2, bias=False),
                nn.BatchNorm2d(out_channels),
                nn.ReLU()
            ),
            nn.Sequential(
                nn.MaxPool2d(3, stride=1, padding=1),
                nn.Conv2d(in_channels, out_channels, 1, bias=False),
                nn.BatchNorm2d(out_channels),
                nn.ReLU()
            ),
        ])
    
    def forward(self, x, alphas):
        """
        x: input
        alphas: [n_ops] architecture weights (logits)
        """
        # Softmax-weighted sum of operations
        weights = F.softmax(alphas, dim=0)
        return sum(w * op(x) for w, op in zip(weights, self.ops))

class DARTSCell(nn.Module):
    """A cell in DARTS (simplified: 4 nodes, 2 inputs)."""
    def __init__(self, in_channels, out_channels):
        super().__init__()
        # Preprocess inputs
        self.preproc0 = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(in_channels, out_channels, 1, bias=False),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(out_channels)
        ) if in_channels != out_channels else nn.Identity()
        self.preproc1 = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(in_channels, out_channels, 1, bias=False),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(out_channels)
        ) if in_channels != out_channels else nn.Identity()
        
        # Edges between nodes (simplified: only 4 edges)
        self.edges = nn.ModuleList([
            MixedOp(out_channels, out_channels) for _ in range(4)
        ])
        # Architecture parameters (logits)
        self.alphas = nn.Parameter(
            torch.zeros(4, 4)  # 4 edges, 4 ops each
        )
    
    def forward(self, s0, s1):
        s0 = self.preproc0(s0)
        s1 = self.preproc1(s1)
        
        # Node 2: takes s0, s1
        n2 = self.edges[0](s0, self.alphas[0]) + self.edges[1](s1, self.alphas[1])
        
        # Node 3: takes s0, s1, n2
        n3 = self.edges[2](s0, self.alphas[2]) + self.edges[3](s1, self.alphas[3])
        
        return n2, n3  # concat for output

# === Bilevel optimization ===
def darts_step(model, train_loader, val_loader, optimizer_weights, optimizer_arch, eta=0.01):
    """
    Single DARTS training step:
    1. Update architecture weights on validation data
    2. Update model weights on training data
    """
    # Step 1: update architecture (validation)
    x_val, y_val = next(iter(val_loader))
    pred_val = model(x_val)
    val_loss = F.cross_entropy(pred_val, y_val)
    
    optimizer_arch.zero_grad()
    val_loss.backward()
    optimizer_arch.step()
    
    # Step 2: update model weights (training)
    x_train, y_train = next(iter(train_loader))
    pred_train = model(x_train)
    train_loss = F.cross_entropy(pred_train, y_train)
    
    optimizer_weights.zero_grad()
    train_loss.backward()
    optimizer_weights.step()
    
    return train_loss.item(), val_loss.item()

# === Discretization (derive final architecture) ===
# After search, keep operation with highest alpha_ij for each edge
# For k operations per node, keep top-2 incoming edges per node
